content: Nash equilibrium: no player can improve their outcome by unilaterally changing strategy. Found by iterating best responses. Pure strategy Nash may not exist; mixed strategy Nash always exists (Nash's theorem). Multiple equilibria possible — selection is the hard problem.

Prisoner's dilemma: individually rational to defect, but mutual cooperation yields better collective outcome. Iterated version: tit-for-tat (cooperate first, then mirror opponent) wins tournaments. Implications for agent collaboration: reward cooperation, punish defection.

Mechanism design (reverse game theory): design rules so self-interested agents produce optimal outcomes. VCG mechanism: each agent pays the externality imposed on others. Ensures truthful reporting of preferences/capabilities. Apply to agent task bidding.

Second-price auction (Vickrey): highest bidder wins, pays second-highest bid. Dominant strategy is to bid true value. Prevents bid shading. ROOT's agents could use Vickrey auctions for task allocation — agents bid their confidence, best one gets the task.

Shannon entropy: H(X) = -sum(p(x) * log2(p(x))). Measures uncertainty/information content. Maximum entropy = log2(n) for n equally likely outcomes. H(coin) = 1 bit. H(die) = 2.585 bits. Use to measure diversity of agent routing decisions.

KL divergence: D_KL(P||Q) = sum(P(x) * log(P(x)/Q(x))). Measures how distribution P differs from reference Q. Asymmetric: D_KL(P||Q) ≠ D_KL(Q||P). Use for agent selection: route to agent with minimum KL divergence from task distribution.

Mutual information: I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y) = sum(p(x,y) * log(p(x,y)/(p(x)*p(y)))). Measures shared information between variables. I=0 means independent. Use for feature selection — captures non-linear dependencies unlike correlation.

Gradient descent variants: SGD (stochastic, noisy but fast), Adam (adaptive learning rates per parameter, most popular), AdamW (Adam with decoupled weight decay, better for LLM training). Learning rate schedule: cosine annealing with warm restarts for best convergence.

Convex optimization: f(lambda*x + (1-lambda)*y) <= lambda*f(x) + (1-lambda)*f(y). Global optimum guaranteed (no local minima traps). Portfolio optimization is convex. Use CVXPY in Python: define objective + constraints, solver finds optimal solution.

Simulated annealing: accept worse solutions with probability exp(-delta/T) where T is temperature. Start hot (accept anything), cool gradually (become greedy). Temperature schedule: T(t) = T_0 * alpha^t, alpha=0.95-0.99. Good for combinatorial optimization.

Genetic algorithms: encode solutions as chromosomes. Selection (tournament/roulette), crossover (combine parents), mutation (random perturbation). Population size 50-200, crossover rate 0.7-0.9, mutation rate 0.01-0.1. Good for multi-modal optimization landscapes.

Pareto optimality: solution is Pareto optimal if no objective can be improved without worsening another. The Pareto frontier contains all non-dominated solutions. NSGA-II finds Pareto frontier efficiently via non-dominated sorting + crowding distance.

Dijkstra's shortest path: O((V+E) log V) with binary heap. Works with non-negative edge weights. Bellman-Ford: O(V*E), handles negative edges, detects negative cycles. For agent communication: find shortest path through agent network for message routing.

Max-flow min-cut theorem (Ford-Fulkerson): maximum flow through a network equals minimum cut capacity. Model task routing as network flow: agents have capacity constraints, edges have bandwidth. Min-cost max-flow gives optimal task distribution.

PageRank: PR(v) = (1-d)/N + d * sum(PR(u)/L(u)) for all u linking to v. Damping factor d=0.85. Iteratively converge. Apply to agent network: agents frequently consulted by other agents get higher rank — identifies informal knowledge hubs.

Community detection — Louvain algorithm: greedily optimizes modularity Q = (1/2m) * sum((A_ij - k_i*k_j/2m) * delta(c_i,c_j)). Fast, hierarchical. Leiden algorithm improves on Louvain by guaranteeing well-connected communities. Use on agent interaction graphs.

Spectral clustering: use k smallest eigenvectors of graph Laplacian L = D - A. Fiedler vector (2nd eigenvector) gives optimal bisection. Captures non-convex cluster shapes that k-means misses. Apply to discover emergent agent specializations.

Small-world networks (Watts-Strogatz): high clustering + short path length. Created by rewiring fraction p of edges in regular lattice. p=0 is regular (high clustering, long paths), p=1 is random (low clustering, short paths), p=0.01-0.1 is small-world (both).

Bayes theorem: P(H|D) = P(D|H) * P(H) / P(D). Sequential updating: posterior from one observation becomes prior for the next. This is how ROOT's agents should update beliefs — incorporate each new data point incrementally, not recompute from scratch.

Law of large numbers: sample mean converges to expected value as n→∞. Central Limit Theorem: sum of n independent variables approaches normal distribution regardless of original distribution (n>30 typically sufficient). Enables statistical inference.

Markov chains: P(X_{t+1} | X_t, X_{t-1}, ...) = P(X_{t+1} | X_t). Future depends only on present state. Stationary distribution pi satisfies pi = pi * P (left eigenvector with eigenvalue 1). Use for modeling market regime transitions.

Monte Carlo methods: estimate quantities by random sampling. Integration: sample points randomly, average function values. Simulation: model system stochastically, run many paths. Variance reduction: importance sampling, antithetic variates, stratified sampling.

Pearl's do-calculus: three rules for computing interventional distributions from observational data given a causal graph. Rule 1: insertion/deletion of observations. Rule 2: action/observation exchange. Rule 3: insertion/deletion of actions.

Structural Causal Models: V_i = f_i(parents_i, U_i). Intervention do(X=x) severs incoming arrows to X. Counterfactual: 'what would have happened if X=x?' requires abduction (infer U from observations), action (set X=x), prediction (compute Y).

NOTEARS: continuous optimization for DAG structure learning. Minimizes ||X - XW||² + lambda*||W||_1 subject to tr(e^(W∘W)) - d = 0 (acyclicity constraint expressed as smooth function). Discovers causal structure from observational data.

Difference-in-Differences: effect = (treated_after - treated_before) - (control_after - control_before). Assumes parallel trends in absence of treatment. Use for measuring impact of strategy changes on ROOT's performance when randomized experiment isn't feasible.

Confirmation bias: tendency to seek information confirming existing beliefs. Counter: actively seek disconfirming evidence, maintain kill thesis for every position, use pre-mortem analysis. Most dangerous bias for traders and decision-makers.

Anchoring bias: over-reliance on first piece of information (the 'anchor'). Adjust: start from base rates not recent events, use multiple reference points, practice explicit de-anchoring. Common in financial forecasting (anchoring to recent prices).

Sunk cost fallacy: continuing investment due to past spending rather than future value. Counter: ask 'would I start this today with what I know now?' If no, cut losses. Applied to ROOT: don't keep underperforming strategies just because development was expensive.

Dunning-Kruger effect: low-skill individuals overestimate ability, high-skill individuals underestimate. Counter: seek calibrated feedback, track prediction accuracy over time (Brier scores), compare self-assessment to outcomes. ROOT's prediction ledger provides this.

Survivorship bias: studying only successes, ignoring failures that 'disappeared'. In trading: backtests on current index constituents ignore delisted companies, inflating returns. In business: studying successful startups ignores identical ones that failed.

Eisenhower matrix: categorize tasks by Urgent×Important. Do (urgent+important), Schedule (important+not urgent), Delegate (urgent+not important), Eliminate (neither). Most value comes from quadrant 2 (important but not urgent) — strategic work.

OODA loop (Boyd): Observe (gather data), Orient (analyze context, update mental model), Decide (choose action), Act (execute). Speed of OODA loop determines competitive advantage. ROOT's proactive engine runs a continuous OODA loop across markets and knowledge.

First principles thinking: decompose problem to fundamental truths, reason up from there. Instead of 'how do competitors do it?' ask 'what does physics/math/economics require?' Elon Musk's approach to rocket costs. Apply to ROOT architecture decisions.

Inversion thinking: instead of asking 'how do I succeed?' ask 'how would I guarantee failure?' then avoid those things. Charlie Munger's approach. For ROOT: 'what would make the hedge fund lose money consistently?' then build safeguards against each.

Second-order thinking: 'and then what?' Every action has consequences that themselves have consequences. First-order: 'lower prices → more customers.' Second-order: '→ competitors match → margin compression → need efficiency.' Think 2-3 steps ahead.

Pre-mortem analysis: before executing a decision, imagine it failed spectacularly. Ask team 'what went wrong?' This surfaces risks that optimism bias hides. Gary Klein's research shows pre-mortem increases risk identification by 30%.

Brier score: BS = (1/N) * sum((forecast_i - outcome_i)²). Decomposition: BS = calibration - resolution + uncertainty. Track each component: calibration = are 70% forecasts right 70%? Resolution = do forecasts vary? Uncertainty = base rate entropy.

Proper scoring rules incentivize honest probability reporting. Log score: S = y*log(p) + (1-y)*log(1-p). More sensitive to extreme probabilities than Brier. Both are strictly proper — forecaster maximizes expected score by reporting true beliefs.

Ensemble forecast combination: optimal weights minimize MSE. Weight = inverse variance: w_i = (1/var_i) / sum(1/var_j). Simple average often beats 'optimal' weights out of sample due to estimation error. Extremize aggregate for crowd under-confidence.

Spaced repetition: review at increasing intervals (1 day, 3 days, 7 days, 14 days, 30 days). Based on Ebbinghaus forgetting curve. Anki implements SM-2 algorithm. ROOT's memory decay cycle should use similar intervals rather than uniform daily decay.

Feynman technique: explain concept in simple terms as if teaching a child. Identify gaps where you can't explain simply. Go back to source material. Simplify again. If you can't explain it simply, you don't understand it well enough.

Deep work (Cal Newport): 4 hours of focused, undistracted work per day is the productive maximum for most people. Schedule deep work blocks in calendar. Eliminate shallow work (email, meetings) from these blocks. Track deep work hours weekly.

Implementation intentions: 'When situation X occurs, I will perform behavior Y.' Research shows 2-3x more likely to follow through than vague goals. Example: 'When market opens, I will check the overnight signals and set stops before doing anything else.'

Zettelkasten method: atomic notes (one idea each) linked to related notes. Notes are permanent (not hierarchical, not by project). Over time, clusters emerge that reveal connections you didn't plan. Digital: Obsidian, Logseq. Core principle: linking > filing.

Systems vs Goals (James Clear): goals set direction, but systems determine progress. 'Goal: lose 10 lbs. System: exercise 30min daily.' Winners and losers have the same goals — the system is the difference. ROOT should focus on improving processes, not just targets.

MECE principle (McKinsey): Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive. When decomposing a problem, categories should not overlap (ME) and should cover everything (CE). Ensures thorough analysis without double-counting. Apply to ROOT's task decomposition.

Steel-manning: construct the strongest possible version of the opposing argument before refuting it. The opposite of straw-manning. Leads to better decisions because you engage with real challenges, not weak versions. Use in MiRo council debates.

OKRs (Objectives and Key Results): Objective = qualitative direction. Key Results = measurable milestones (2-5 per objective). Score 0-1.0 quarterly. Target 0.7 (stretch but achievable). If always scoring 1.0, goals aren't ambitious enough.

Active inference for agent decision-making: minimize expected free energy G = ambiguity + risk. Ambiguity = uncertainty about consequences. Risk = divergence from preferred outcomes. Agent naturally explores (reduces ambiguity) and exploits (reduces risk).
